An endured satellite-on-a-chip for deep space missions  
Eduardo Augusto Bezerra 
 
UFSC, CTC, PPGEEL, SpaceLab, Campus Trindade, Florianópolis, SC 
Tel: +55 (48) 3721 2359, http://gse.ufsc.br/bezerra/ 
 
Comitê Assessor: ME – Microeletrônica 
Área de Conhecimento: Projeto 
 
Abstract 
 
The research group under the coordination of the author of the proposal was one of the first in the 
world to develop activities related to the movement called new space. The activities in the space field by the 
proponent started in 1993, during the master's degree carried out at UFRGS. At the time the research 
activities were related to embedded electronics of conventional satellites. In the early 2000s, shortly after 
completing the PhD at the Space Science Centre at the University of Sussex in England, research and 
development of electronic systems for use in the space environment began, through the use of COTS 
(Commercial Off-The-Shelf). The first modules based on COTS were used in the design of the FloripaSat-1 
satellite, which was one of the first representatives of new space in Latin America. With a total mass of 
965g, the satellite entirely designed and developed at SpaceLab was launched in December 2019, going into 
operation on the same day of launch, even before the other Brazilian satellite launched on the same rocket, 
the CBERS-4A with a mass of 1,700 kg. In recent years, activities have been carried out at SpaceLab in 
order to concentrate the functionalities of the modules of the service platform of a satellite on a single chip 
(Satellite-on-a-Chip, SAToC), aiming at missions in the context of new space. These activities will continue 
to be carried out in the coming years, but also considering more reliable components, with radiation 
tolerance features. Our first demand for radiation hardened devices for a new space project came from a 
mission funded by the European Space Agency (ESA), aiming not only for greater processing power, but 
also reconfiguration in orbit, and use in higher orbit missions, as well as the interplanetary ones. 
Unfortunately, in Brazil we still do not have adequate facilities to manufacture radiation hardened FPGA 
devices to be used in the implementation of a SAToC. The research and development of national embedded 
solutions for the space field is a major SpaceLab mission, and one of the goals of this proposal is to 
collaborate with other researchers in the area of microelectronics at UFSC and partner institutions, targeting  
the design of radiation hardened FPGA devices. The network of partners will work in a fabless way, and the 
final design will be fabricated abroad. It is not a goal of this proposal to develop the whole FPGA, but to 
define the expected requirements of a radiation hardened device for use in space, as well as the radiation 
protection strategies to be used in the project at the HDL level. It is also intended to establish in the 
laboratory a culture in the study of protection strategies at the technology level. This study will be carried out 
by researchers in the VLSI and materials area of the PPGEEL at UFSC. At the application level, the proposal 
aims to continue the ongoing research at SpaceLab in the area of designing a satellite on a chip (SAToC). 
Contextualising the focus of the research, the final objective concerns the use of FPGAs as a target device for 
the synthesis of the components necessary to carry out the activities of on-board processing of the service 
module of a satellite. Since 2004, the author has received financial and institutional support from institutions 
such as the Brazilian Space Agency (AEB) and CNPq, under the supervision of INPE, to carry out research 
aimed at generating solutions for the implementation of the on-board computing system for future Brazilian 
space missions. Among the main contributions of the research developed in the last 15 years, the following 
stand out: training of human resources at the undergraduate, master, doctoral, and post-doctoral levels; 
publications in conferences and scientific journals; organisation of scientific conferences; research and 
development in the area of embedded systems for on-board processing; international exchanges of students

5 
 
and researchers; and satellite development. A feature shared by these contributions is the search for 
reliability improvement, and acceleration of the processing of embedded systems in space applications. 
These improvements are usually obtained through resource replication, that is, use of additional modules to 
improve reliability (redundancy), and processing power. However, replicating resources typically results in 
increased power consumption. In space applications, where energy is a scarce resource, replication aimed at 
improving reliability and processing power needs to be carefully designed. This compromise between 
reliability and processing speed is one of the major research challenges to be addressed in this proposal. The 
main objectives to be achieved include: research of tools, technologies and architectures to enable the design 
of a satellite on-board computing system on a single chip, considering the requirements of space applications 
(eg reliability, real-time, processing power, energy efficiency); and system validation at the software and 
hardware levels (HDL), using hardware/software co-design strategies, according to space field requirements. 
The case study consists of embedding the components of the service platform of SpaceLab’s satellites on a 
single chip for future use in ongoing space missions. The components of the service platform in question 
include the On-Board Data Handling (OBDH), Telemetry and Telecommand (TT&C), and the Electric 
Power System (EPS), using technologies and design methodologies aiming at greater processing power, 
reliability improvement, and reduced energy consumption. Thus, another objective of the present project is to 
continue the research work conducted in recent years, more specifically, with regard to the integration of a 
satellite's on-board computer system in a single chip solution. In addition to important academic and 
scientific results, it is interesting to emphasise the importance of this research for the Brazilian 
microelectronics program, as it will generate not only intellectual property cores (IP cores) for the various 
components of a satellite's on-board computer, but it will also help starting a new investigation area at UFSC 
in the field of integrated circuits with national technology targeting aerospace applications. From the space 
point of view, this research will result in technological independence for future Brazilian space missions. 
 
Keywords: Satellite-on-a-chip, FPGA, Reliability, Radiation-hardened

1. Introduction 
Technological advances allow on-board systems becoming more and more autonomous, not 
only for the navigation and attitude control activities of a spacecraft, but also in the pre-processing 
of data to be transmitted to ground stations. In the big data era, on-board pre-processing is a de facto 
need in modern satellite platforms [HAN15][OBE15][LIM14][WAL11][PIN10]. The availability of 
low power high resolution sensors for remote sensing, high data rate instruments, a variety of 
cameras generating a huge amount of pixels to be compressed, among other data intensive 
operations, result in the need of multi-processed architectures, similar to the ones used in ground 
level applications [AGRA08]. However, the reliability and energy requirements in the space 
environment are stricter, placing the research on this type of parallel processing architecture at the 
knowledge frontier [BUT15]. Reliability improvement and system processing acceleration features 
are often achieved through redundancy. The implementation of traditional redundancy strategies 
usually results in an increase in the energy consumption. In space applications, where energy is a 
scarce resource, both reliability and processing power improvements must be carefully designed 
and this compromise is a major research challenge conducted at the SpaceLab, UFSC. 
The idea of having a whole satellite's functionality implemented in a single chip (satellite-
on-a-chip, SAToC) is not new. Considering the small satellites field, Abhay Joshi gave an interview 
in 1994 describing his plans for a startup company to work on this topic [JOS94]. Also, 
Vladimirova's group from Surrey Space Centre, England, developed the basic architecture of a 
SAToC, to implement a CCSDS based communication system, targeting an FPGA device 
[BAR06][VLA07].  
 
As shown in Figure 1, the group from Surrey has proposed also an ASIC implementation for 
a SAToC, including all the basic subsystems of a satellite: Telemetry, Tracking & Control (TT&C); 
Electrical Power System (EPS); Thermal control; On-Board Data Handling (OBDH); Attitude 
Determination and Control System (ADCS); and Payload [BAR06]. While the SAToC in Figure 1 
is more "general" than the CCSDS based one, it is clear that the former can be used in an actual 
mission, as the main communication system of a satellite. The SAToC shown in Figure 1 can be 
seen more as a proof-of-concept, than as a solution for a real space mission. It can be used in an 
actual space mission, but only if the mission has very specific requirements (e.g. energy 
requirements, data rates, payload processing capabilities, ...). Considering that, the "generic" 
SAToC shown in Figure 1 becomes a solution for just very specific missions. 
 
At UFSC, in the SpaceLab, several modules of a satellite have been developed. There are 
modules to be used in sub-systems as EPS, OBDH, ADCS, TT&C, Thermal control, and the 
payloads. The research and development activities related to these modules are conducted by two 
different groups, at different abstraction levels, and employing quite different technologies. One of 
the groups works in the design of the subsystems at the Printed Circuit Board (PCB) level, as shown 
in Figure 2. The other group focus is on the migration of these modules functionalities to a satellite-
on-a-chip implemented using FPGAs or ASICs. This is a quite interesting challenge, and the first 
step was to have a "satellite-on-a-board", instead of a "satellite-on-a-chip".  The target board is 
based on FPGA technology. The FPGA device holds the digital modules, and the remaining SAToC 
modules, including analog devices, external memories, among others, are placed on the PCB, 
around the main FPGA. An important goal of the proposed project is to conduct the research of 
multiprocessor system-on-chip (MPSoC) architectures, and the development of a single chip 
solution for on-board systems considering space applications requirements (reliability, real-time 
operation, processing power, energy consumption efficiency). In space systems, parallel processing

9 
 
is usually employed for CPU bound applications as, for instance, image processing. However, the 
new technology used in sensors and actuators, result in advanced attitude control systems, 
demanding also higher processing power. There are no more than 10 countries in the world 
possessing the technology to develop an attitude control system. This is a strategic technology, as it 
can be used not only to control satellites, but also to guide rockets and missiles. For this reason, the 
International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), from the United States of America, restricts the 
commercialization of parts and electronics components to space programs which intend to launch 
their satellites using rockets from proscribed countries as, for instance, China. Recently, Thales-
Alenia had to cancel a contract to launch a satellite in a Chinese rocket, as they employed ITAR 
protected components in their satellite's design. The result is that Thales-Alenia had to look for a 
western launcher with a budget similar to the Chinese one. 
 
Figure 1. Satellite-on-a-chip ASIC from Surrey Space Centre [BAR06]. 
 
These ITAR restrictions motivated the on-going research at UFSC in order to develop a 
single chip solution for space applications, targeting FPGA devices. While some radiation-hardened 
and military microprocessors, and other electronic components, have their commercialization 
restricted under the ITAR regulations, "blank" FPGAs can be used to implement soft-core versions 
of such components. The idea is to adapt existent architecture, in order to comply with the 
requirements of a chosen application. After several years of experience in the research and 
development of computer architectures for space applications, it has been noticed a trend towards 
the need for a single chip solution of an on-board computer capable of performing not only attitude 
control, but also the remaining satellite functionalities, which can be accomplished through a 
combination of an MPSoC architecture and hardware components (HDL).

10 
 
The porting of software and hardware components from legacy PCB designs to single chip 
FPGA based implementations is an interesting engineering defy, but it is not exactly a major 
challenge. It is indeed a time consuming task, and it requires special skills, technology knowledge, 
and advanced development tools. The research and development of satellites' hardware/software 
components to be embedded in a single chip is a major research challenge, as well as the tuning of 
these components, in order to comply with the strict space standards. Another important research 
challenge is the software and hardware (HDL) validation process. There are well-defined 
procedures for the validation of hardware (PCBs and electronic circuits), and also for software 
(software test strategies). However, the validation of a hardware description language (HDL) based 
system is a real research challenge, as an HDL design is neither software (does not result in an 
executable binary code), nor hardware (the resulting netlist placement/routing may change at each 
synthesis run). In computer architectures for space applications, dependability figures need to be 
carefully analysed and improved. Another scientific challenge at the architecture level is the reliable 
migration from a mono-processed (System-on-a-Chip, SoC) to a multi-processed (MPSoC) 
architecture, aiming for processing capability improvement with no expressive energy consumption 
increase. This may be a topic already investigated in other fields, but the requirements of critical 
real-time applications, such as in the aerospace industry, makes this a very challenging research 
topic.  
 
Figure 2. SpaceLab’s flatsat featuring the EPS, TT&C, OBDH, and payloads developed at the lab. 
 
Taking into account the aforementioned technological advances, the main objective of this 
proposal is the research and development of a satellite-on-a-chip architecture for on-board 
systems considering space applications requirements (reliability, real-time operation, 
processing power, energy consumption efficiency). Other objectives include:

11 
 
● To speed up system performance, through multi-processed architectures, with no major 
impact in reliability figures and power consumption. 
● Reliability improvement, through system validation at several abstraction levels, 
according to standards from the space industry. 
● To contribute with Brazilian microelectronics groups working in the conception of an 
endured FPGA device in a fabless way. 
● Conceive a system ready for in-orbit validation (IoV), which will result in high quality 
scientific production in the field of microelectronics systems for space applications. 
In the remainder of this text these objectives and research challenges are further discussed, 
as well as the proposed architecture and the methodology to be followed. There is a list of activities 
needed to accomplish the objectives, a discussion on the relevance and scientific impact of the 
proposal, and also a description of previous research activities related to the proposed project. 
 
2. Proposed research 
In recent years, the major on-going development activities at SpaceLab are related to the 
satellites GOLDS-UFSC (a.k.a. FloripaSat-2), Catarina-A1a, Catarina-A1b, Aldebaran-I, and 
GOMX-5. The team members work in all aspects of these satellites, including software, hardware, 
boards, mechanical, and thermal aspects. There are also other on-going activities in the lab as, for 
instance, orbit calculations, radiation studies and other space related issues. 
These development activities are a direct result of the research activities conducted in the 
past, and at present. The proposed satellite-on-a-chip research topic has been partially investigated 
in Master Dissertations and PhD Theses under the supervision of the author of this proposal. It is an 
ever changing topic that deserves a continuous investigation in the state-of-the-art and the recent 
findings. In addition, the conception of a radiation-hardened FPGA architecture has also been 
investigated in the past, as this sort of technology is essential for space missions. 
The proposed satellite-on-a-chip is composed of several Intellectual Property (IP) cores 
written in a Hardware Description Language (HDL), and it is endured through design techniques at 
HDL level, and also when using a radiation-hardened FPGA device. Next there is a description of 
the proposed research, considering the satellite-on-a-chip on-going work, and also the target 
radiation-hardened FPGA architecture. 
 
2.1. Radiation-hardened FPGA device 
The IP cores of the proposed satellite-on-a-chip have been developed targeting several 
FPGA families, from different vendors. Some of them are just COTS, with no specific radiation 
protection features. In these cases, fault tolerant mechanisms were defined and implemented 
[MAR15][MAR18][TRA18][VIL18][VIL19]. These mechanisms are necessary in order for the 
system to survive in the harsh space environment, when no reliable FPGA devices are available. 
The resulting effects of radiation on FPGAs are diverse, and those classified as Total Ionising Dose 
(TID) and Single-Event Effects (SEE) are of great interest to this research. The Ionising radiation is 
generated in space in three ways: Solar Wind and Solar Cosmic Rays (SCR); Galactic Cosmic Rays 
(GCR); and Van Allen Belts (The Van Allen Belts). The source of Galactic Cosmic Rays are 
galaxies and stars, and they are the most intense among the energetic particles, being difficult to 
protect. SEE are of special interest as they have strong effects on digital circuits. There are several

12 
 
forms of SEE, including the Single-Event Upset (SEU), the Single-Event Latchup (SEL), and the 
Single-Event Transient (SET). SEUs represent the main cause of stuck-at faults, when a bit is kept 
in logic level 0 or logic level 1. This type of fault manifests itself mainly in SRAM-based devices, 
such as memories and FPGAs. A SEU occurs when a high-energy particle collides with a memory 
cell, losing energy, and resulting in a densely ionised region. This will force the memory cell to 
remain in a state, 0 or 1, and consequently, stuck-at faults are the best option to model the errors 
that occur due to this type of radiation. In the case of SEE, the effects are more immediate, as a 
result of the flow of energetic particles through the electronic systems. On the other hand, TID 
presents long-term effects, as a consequence of the long-term exposure of the electronic systems to 
radiation.  
The detection and tolerance of these types of faults is one of the goals of this research. 
Considering these radiation menaces, flash or antifuse FPGAs must be used instead of SRAM-based 
FPGAs as they are more susceptible to Single-Event Upset (SEU) as well as Electromagnetic 
Interference (EMI). This fact has particular importance for SEU and EMI as the device’s sensitivity 
increases with technology and power-supply scaling. Note that technology scaling has increased the 
device clock frequency and driven down power supply voltage, which consequently renders devices 
more sensitive to external perturbations. Fault tolerance techniques may be sufficient to protect 
SRAM-based devices, when considering Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and short time missions, but when 
considering interplanetary space missions, or even higher orbit missions, in these cases radiation-
hardened devices must be employed. These devices are conceived to tolerate not only SEE but also 
TID, and this higher energy radiation induces changes in the chemical and physical structure of 
materials, and in living beings they can be lethal. Their interaction has a corpuscular feature. The 
effects of the passage of electromagnetic radiation through matter results in: heating; luminescence; 
chemical effects; ionisation; and nuclear excitation. When passing through matter, a beam of 
electromagnetic radiation loses intensity due to absorption and diffusion. Absorption occurs when a 
photon is captured, either by an atom or a molecule, causing the system to transition to a higher 
energy level. Diffusion is simply a ray that has deviated from its trajectory. With all these effects of 
radioactivity, the circuit is more susceptible to electromagnetic interference caused by other 
electronics onboard the satellite. This results in large electromagnetic interference both radiated and 
conducted in adjacent circuits. The circuit over time accumulates ionising radiation, and it is 
affected in several ways. There is an increase in leakage current, corrosion of the board tracks, and 
changes in the internal structure of the transistors of the integrated circuits. 
In space missions, considered as “long term”, hardware and software modules need to be 
designed considering that post-launch maintenance activities are usually impractical, or have a very 
high cost. They also need to take into account the fact that the space environment is quite hostile 
and will certainly cause problems for the system. In traditional microprocessor-based space 
systems, fault tolerance techniques are used in order to keep vital systems operational, such as those 
responsible for communicating with ground stations and navigation and attitude determination 
systems. Computer systems need to “adapt” to new situations, in order to remain operational even 
in the event of problems. From the hardware point of view, this adaptation occurs by isolating the 
faulty modules and, if possible, by activating spare modules. At the software level, the adaptation 
can be identified, basically, when the microprocessor starts executing a new program to replace the 
defective module. This new program could be stored aboard the satellite, or it could be loaded from 
a ground station.

13 
 
Strategies for designing radiation-hardened devices will be investigated, in order to help 
partners develop a library of components to be used by commercial EDA tools. Thus, it will be 
possible to design the digital blocks of an FPGA in a hardware description language, using the 
components provided by the EDA tool. The developer will be able to decide, during development, 
whether to use conventional components or components designed with radiation tolerance 
characteristics (special flip-flops, logic gates, ...). The netlist produced by the EDA tool can be used 
in the physical design and fabrication of a radiation-hardened FPGA. Thus, both the manufacturing 
process and functional aspects of the components will be considered in the proposed research. 
The research aims to cooperate with other groups working in the design of radiation-
hardened library components, targeting the conception of an ITAR free Brazilian FPGA. An 
important motivation for this research is the difficulty in acquiring qualified components for space 
applications in the international market. This difficulty is due not only to the lack of manufacturers, 
but also due to trade restrictions in this field. The import restrictions of electronic components for 
space and defence applications have increased, being of strategic importance that the country 
develops this technology, to avoid greater further consequences to the space program, which would 
directly impact in several areas, mainly in the monitoring of the natural resources of the Amazon 
region. 
A similar approach has been taken by other countries, in order to have their own radiation-
hardened FPGA solution. This is the case of France where Nanoxplore, a fabless company 
developed a family of radiation-hardened FPGAs. The BRAVE family of FPGAs has three 
members: NG-Medium; NG-Large; and NG-Ultra. They are manufactured by STM, also in France, 
and they are ITAR free. The SEU tolerance is a combination of several factors: the process 
technology (C65 Space Process); the circuit design (RHBD Skyrob library); and the device density 
(Nanoxplore full custom design). All logic of BRAVE FPGAs is hardened by design using, for 
instance, EDAC in the register files, DICE in the configuration memories and flip-flops, DMR in 
the clock tree, and TMR in other logic cells (Skyrob library). There are additional integrity checks 
in the embedded configuration memory. There are interesting design decisions as, for instance, 
DICE in the memory cells and DFFs. This technique results in more transistors per memory cell, 
but it is quite suitable for SEU radiation effects as a cell memory can only flip a bit if two channels 
switch at the same time. So, an energy particle striking a cell cannot flip the bit. The NG-Medium 
has been designed, verified, manufactured using 65nm CMOS technology from STM, and it has 
35k LUTs. The NG-Large has 140k LUTs in 65nm CMOS, and the NG-Ultra has 600k LUTs using 
28nm technology. Nanoxplore also provides NanoXmap, which is the synthesis tool for the 
BRAVE FPGAs. The FPGAs have special features for space applications. For instance, they have 
embedded Spacewire physical interfaces, as this type of protocol is largely used in European Space 
Agency missions. The NG-Large and NG-Ultra have hard IP core ARM processors connected to the 
logic blocks through AXI interfaces. 
The main Nanoxplore customers for the BRAVE FPGA family are Airbus Defence & 
Space, and ThalesAlenia Space. 
The goal of this research topic is not to develop the full component library for producing a 
radiation-hardened FPGA, but to create a culture in SpaceLab aiming the support to other groups to 
develop libraries, in order to have a fabless company in Brazil, similar to NanoXplore, producing 
radiation-hardened FPGAs for the Brazilian Space Program, the defence industry, and also for 
export. Target groups in Brazil are Santa Maria Design House and Chipus which already have 
projects with UFSC.

14 
 
 
2.2. Satellite-on-a-chip 
The basic concept behind the satellite-on-a-chip is similar to other technologies as, for 
instance, the system-on-chip, or the multi-processor system-on-a-chip. In these technologies, self-
contained modules (also called as cores, or intellectual property cores - IP cores), are connected 
together in order to implement a more complete system. The complete system will also need an 
interconnection infrastructure that must be designed/chosen according to the system requirements. 
A software platform could be used in order to help the designers put together the system modules, 
but the research goal is not to develop a new one. The idea is to adapt an existent architecture, in 
order to comply with the requirements of the chosen application. 
The basic functionalities in order to have a single chip solution to implement the on-board 
systems of a satellite include: 
● Communications - the satellite must have a way to receive commands from a ground 
station, and to send back telemetry (service and payload data). 
● Attitude determination and control - there must be a way for the satellite to take actions 
regarding its trajectory, which could be under the control of the ground segment, or 
automatically/semi-automatically. 
● Energy management - there must be strategies for the energy harvesting, storage, and 
distribution to the remaining modules of the system. 
● Processing - All the information flow of the satellite must be pre-processed, in order to 
have a proper management of all modules. Important tasks to be executed by the 
processing module include housekeeping, and payload management. 
● Thermal control - The satellite is exposed to a very harsh environment, and there must be 
strategies to manage the thermal aspects of the on-board systems. 
● Payload - satellites are used mainly to collect, process, and forward data. There are 
different types of payloads, and typical satellite data include images and audio. 
 
However, considering the available technology, a generic solution targeting all these 
functionalities is not feasible yet. As a result, the proposed architecture will be able to provide all 
these functionalities but, for each mission, just some of them will be the main ones, according to the 
mission requirements. For instance, a 5G telecommunications driven mission, is going to be based 
on such communication modules, and a precision navigation based mission, is going to have ADCS 
modules. 
In the previous period of the PQ scholarship, the Attitude Determination and Control System 
(ADCS) component of the SAToC has not been finished. It was the research topic of a PhD student, 
who decided to change the subject. However, the new space missions under development at 
SpaceLab have a high demand for this component. A mission with no strict pointing and positioning 
requirements may not need to have an active ADCS. This is the case of past SpaceLab missions as, 
for instance, the FloripaSat-1 satellite, where a passive attitude control system was sufficient to 
keep the antennas pointing towards the Ground Stations. In the new satellite missions, an active 
ADCS will be necessary, not only for the new directional antennas, but also for the imaging 
sensors. The ADCS is the most complex component of the proposed SAToC, and its design 
received the support from researchers from Ukraine during their visit to UFSC in 2021. Their 
technology used in the design of an ADCS for rockets has been adapted to be used in the attitude

15 
 
determination and control system of a small satellite. The complexity associated with the control 
software, the target microprocessor, the involved sensors and actuators, and the respective 
interfaces, results in major difficulties to accomplish this module. The ADCS is a continuous work 
in the research group, and the preliminary results to be obtained in the following years will be 
published in conferences and scientific journals. In Figure 2 there is a block diagram of the 
reference system, and some of the components shown in the figure have already been implemented 
at SpaceLab. 
 
 
Figure 3. An OBC, implementing an ADCS with its sensors and actuators. 
 
In order to process the data from all sensors in the proposed ADCS, and to activate all the 
actuators, the solution was to employ a multi-processed architecture. In a space system, this 
architecture must have special features, aiming for processing capability improvement with no 
expressive energy consumption increase. This may be a topic already investigated in other fields, 
but the requirements of critical real-time applications, such as in the aerospace industry, makes this 
a very challenging research topic. In the previous scholarship period, two different multi-processed 
architectures have been implemented using FPGAs as the target platform. One of them is in use in 
the Advanced Payload Processors (APPs) developed at SpaceLab for the GOMX-5 Mission 
[BEZ21]. GOMX-5 is a satellite from Gomspace, and in this mission SpaceLab is funded by the 
European Space Agency. The goal is to employ the FPGA-based payload developed at SpaceLab in 
the GOMX-5 mission. Two versions of the multi-processed architecture have been implemented, 
one of them with the RISC-V IP core, and the other one employing a much simpler processor core 
provided by a company from the UK. The RISC-V version offered more processing power, but 
UFSC’s team is facing difficulties to interface some of the peripherals as the Spacewire 
communication module. For this reason, the IP cores provided by the UK company were adopted in 
the first stable version of APPs. The conception of the multiprocessor system-on-chip (MPSoC) 
architecture, to serve as a base building block of a high performance on-board computer for use in 
satellites, is a challenging research topic. At the moment, there are two PhD students at SpaceLab 
using the RISC-V processor in their respective Theses, targeting an improved version of the 
proposed multi-processed architecture, to be used in the ADCS.

16 
 
The FPGA technology has been chosen as it is a good option to cope with ITAR issues, 
allowing the implementation of systems with the same functionality as restricted components. A 
drawback is the complexity associated with the development in HDL languages, when compared to 
the traditional programming languages. In situations where there is no other option, this drawback is 
leveraged. Another drawback is that depending on the application as, for instance, when considering 
embedded soft-cores, FPGAs may not deliver the same performance levels as their counterpart 
physical microprocessors. However, there are studies showing that, on the other hand, FPGAs have 
better energy consumption levels than CPUs and GPGPUs. In order to improve even more the 
consumption figures in FPGA based systems, and taking advantage of a soft-processor HDL source 
code availability, the whole architecture of a SAToC should be made energy aware. RISC-V is a 
well structured soft-processor, but there is still room for improvements targeting better consumption 
performance. For example, its pipeline could be reformulated in order to mitigate unnecessary 
activities. More advanced architectural structures could be added to improve instruction-level 
control, reducing activities and clock pulses in the system. In order to avoid code compatibility 
issues, although not recommended, the compiler itself could be modified to generate code more 
cache optimised, and with a better register usage, always concerned about this type of battery-
driven SAToC implementation. When choosing between performance improvement or energy 
efficiency, there is always a compromise. Simulations should be performed for each application to 
identify what is more energy efficient: employing an MPSoC architecture aiming the pre-processing 
to minimise the amount of data to be transmitted to ground stations; or performing a minimal pre-
processing, using a more traditional approach, which will result in a large amount of data to be 
transmitted. The use of several cores (or several processors) is necessary in order to achieve higher 
system processing acceleration figures. This type of redundancy is also useful to improve the 
system's reliability. Traditional structural and temporal redundancy implemented, for instance, 
through TMR, superscalar and pipeline based architectures, result in an increase in the energy 
consumption. In space applications, where energy is a scarce resource, both reliability and 
processing power improvements must be carefully designed and this is a major research challenge 
to be conducted at the SpaceLab/UFSC. 
The RISC-V based MPSoC is used also in the processing tasks of the satellite’s 
communications systems. The telecommand and telemetry CCSDS module implemented inVHDL 
at SpaceLab was conceived, originally, to be used in the Multi-Mission Platform of the Brazilian 
Space Program. Afterwards, it has been downsized in a PhD Thesis, in order to be used in a small 
satellite [SAL20][SAL22]. It is a complex system as it implements a full version of a CCSDS 
recommendation for space data communications, and it has been used for several years, in order to 
be qualified as the telecommand (TC) and telemetry (TM) system of a satellite. Both, the TC and 
TM modules, have a coding layer in charge of encoding and decoding data. In space applications, 
CCSDS recommends Reed-Solomon (RS) for TM encoding and Bose-Chaudhuri-Hocquenghem 
(BCH) algorithm for TC encoding. The implemented TC/TM module received the denomination 
UTMC. Another on-going work at the SpaceLab regarding the communications module of the 
satellite, is the Software Defined Radio (SDR). Important motivations to use software-defined radio 
in a satellite include the flexibility introduced by this technology, the short development time, and 
lower costs when compared to more traditional approaches. In [TSO17], the authors describe their 
library of reusable modules implemented in VHDL. The library can be used by SDR designers, in 
order to select the most appropriate ones according to their application requirements. In the library 
there are the following DSP cores: Finite Impulse Response (FIR); Infinite Impulse Response (IIR); 
Fast Fourier Transform/Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (FFT/IFFT); and Digital Down Converter

17 
 
(DDC) algorithms. In order to have a complete SDR implementation, there must also be a processor 
(for the software execution), an interconnection infrastructure to connect the DSP cores and the 
processor(s), and analog components outside the FPGA (LNA, PGA, AD, DA, ...). 
The DSP IP cores will be used together with the MPSoC in the implementation of the next 
generation of communication systems to be employed in the constellation of satellites under 
development at SpaceLab. In these constellations, new standards will be used such as the 5G in 
order to address requirements and performance needs of machine-type communications (MTC). 
There are three services defined in the 5G system concept: Extreme Mobile Broadband (xMBB); 
Massive  MTC (mMTC); and Ultra-reliable MTC (uMTC). All of them could be used in space 
applications but, for instance, xMBB targets applications with high volume and data rates as, for 
instance, augmented reality systems. Reliability is an important aspect in space applications, but the 
uMTC service targets systems such as, for instance, remote surgery. The mMTC service seems a 
good candidate for a small satellite mission, as it could be used in a massive data collection system. 
This service aims to define the rules for having massive deployments of low-cost battery-powered 
sensors and actuators, remote controlled and readable. The 5G will provide connectivity solutions 
for a huge number of devices, and it could be a good satellite control/monitoring application, as an 
actual Internet-of-Things space system. Considering the on-growing IoT trending, low power wide 
area networks (LPWAN) have become a popular low-rate long-range radio communication 
technology. Namely, Sigfox, LoRa and NB-IoT are the three leading LWPAN technologies for 
large-scale IoT deployment. In case of the target application, NB-IoT offers benefits in terms of 
latency and quality of service. In order to reduce the amount of consumed energy, NB-IoT end 
devices are in sleep mode most of the time outside operation. NB-IoT can be implemented through 
an LTE carrier, and can be ported to 5G in near future since 3GPP has agreed that the technology 
will continue evolving as part of the 5G specifications. At the moment there is a TC/TM board 
based on LoRa developed at SpaceLab under testing, and Master students working with NP-IoT and 
also LoRa in order to have sufficient background to port the algorithms to FPGA and C to embed 
them in the proposed SAToC. 
 
3. Methodology 
 
The complex aspects involved in a research project in the field of computer architecture for 
space applications, will demand not only specialised and well-trained people, but also special 
equipment and installations. The author of this proposal is the head of the Space Technology 
Research Laboratory (SpaceLab) at UFSC, and also a permanent member in the Graduate Program 
in Electrical Engineering (PPGEEL), and in the Graduate Program in Electronic Systems 
Engineering (PPGESE), both at UFSC. A full satellite mission (FloripaSat-1) has been developed in 
the SpaceLab facilities, and several subsystems have been designed as a result of PPGEEL and 
PPGESE theses. At the moment, there are five on-going satellite missions at SpaceLab, 
simultaneously, which demonstrates our organisation and capability to develop this sort of work.  
The Space Systems Research Laboratory (SpaceLab) has facilities to support the research 
and development of space systems, including the ground segment (satellite tracking and control 
stations) and the space segment (satellite software, hardware and electro-mechanical systems). The 
basic infrastructure includes: computers; software (PCB design and simulation; orbit simulation; 
CAD); development kits (FPGAs, microprocessors, software defined radio - SDR, …); power

18 
 
supply; waveform generators; test equipments (oscilloscopes, logic analyzers); SMD soldering 
station; 3D printer; home-made test equipments specific for satellite modules tests (Sun simulator; 
Environment Test Chamber), among others. In addition to basic infrastructure, the laboratory also 
concentrates the knowledge necessary for the development and operation of a complete space 
mission. 
In this proposal, two lines of research with different characteristics are described. In the 
research and development of a radiation-hardened FPGA device, the objective is not to develop the 
FPGA itself, but to create at SpaceLab an environment to support other groups in Brazil working on 
similar initiatives. The research at UFSC is focused on the development of basic libraries to be used 
in EDA tools for the generation of radiation-hardened technology. On the other hand, the work 
related to the research and development of components to create a SAToC can be entirely 
developed in SpaceLab, since there is already a well-established culture in the laboratory after about 
two decades of work in the area. Furthermore, considering the proposed SAToC, it will be ready for 
a qualification flight on-board a satellite, after the full system integration, and when: all HDL 
descriptions have been through the validation strategies; the reliability improvement techniques are 
in place; the OBC subsystems software is properly ported to the new MPSoC processor cores; the 
operating system is scheduling the parallel system tasks according to the mission requirements; and 
all system requirements (i.e. reliability, real-time operation, processing power, energy consumption 
efficiency) have been met. 
From the methodology point of view, a feature shared by both research topics is that the 
strict requirements found in space applications, make the design methodology heavily based on 
reliability improvement, through verification and validation procedures. Another methodological 
aspect is that the work is conducted with the help of Undergraduate, Master and PhD students 
developing their research in topics related to this proposal. There are strong links also with other 
laboratories in Brazil and abroad, with a quite dynamic exchange of researchers and students. 
The methodology aspects considered at SpaceLab missions can be organised in three main 
groups. At the component level, the major concern is the “Reliability improvement”. At the system 
level, the methodology is heavily based on “Verification and validation”. Finally, at the System 
Engineering level, the “Project management” discipline is in place in all missions. These 
methodology aspects are discussed next. 
 
3.1. Reliability improvement 
The research activities in the group target strategies for performance improvement, with low 
increase in power consumption, and reliability enhancement. There are also strategies to validate 
the designs, which are highly dependent on the target technology. All works described in this 
section have been developed under my supervision. For instance, a strategy for fault injection 
acceleration in HDL designs has been developed in a recent PhD Thesis [FER16]. A previous 
version of the strategy had been used in a fault tolerant on-board computer based on the LEON3 
processor [SIL12]. The strategy uses safety requisites, described in standards such as the 
automotive ISO 26262, and the aerospace DO-254. The standards provide guidance to ensure that 
the requirements are implemented, and fault injection is highly recommended for the functional 
verification of safety mechanisms. According to the safety standards, an exhaustive analysis is not 
required, but evidence of best effort through the diagnostic coverage assessment needs to be 
provided when performing quantitative evaluation of hardware architectural metrics. The thesis

19 
 
explored advanced verification solutions in order to build a methodology to accelerate the 
diagnostic coverage assessment. The fault injection strategy uses a gate-level netlist as input. The 
output is a reduced list of faults to be used in fault injection campaigns.  
In any case, when using HDL based implementations targeting FPGAs in critical 
applications, special attention should be given to system reliability assurance. There are several 
reliability improvement strategies available in the literature, and the starting point for any strategy is 
the definition of the fault model to be adopted. In order to define the fault model, it is necessary first 
to understand the implementation technology available. So far, the delivered systems to the 
Brazilian Space Program targeted antifuse FPGAs, but SRAM based FPGAs are also a research 
target, as in both technologies the FPGA's configuration memory is very flexible allowing on-the-
fly modifications in on-board functionalities. An important research problem is the SRAM 
technology's susceptibility to radiation. The main fault model related to SRAM FPGAs is the stuck-
at, which is used to model faults resulted from single-event upsets (SEUs), i.e. faults where a bit is 
kept in the 0 or 1 logic levels. Papers describing past research work in this field conducted in the 
group have been published [BEZ99][BEZ00][BEZ01][BEZ01a]. 
Considering this kind of fault model, a fault tolerance technique based on temporal 
redundancy was proposed in a PhD Thesis [VIL19]. Figure 10 shows the block diagram of the 
proposed strategy. This work is a result of a cooperation with researchers from the UNSW in 
Sydney, Australia. In their work, they propose the usage of the technique in a home made processor, 
implemented in ASIC [LI13][LI13a]. A new version of the technique has been developed in the 
PhD research, targeting the RISC-V processor, configured in the MPSoC architecture discussed in 
Section 2.2. Some important features of the implemented strategy include: 
● There are no modifications in the compiler. 
● All the modifications are performed in the VHDL implementation of the RISC-V 
processor, mainly in the pipeline implementation. Even after the modifications, the new 
RISC-V is still fully compatible with the original ISA. 
● The Checkpoint/Recovery module proposed in [VIL19] has been added for on-line 
monitoring of the programs' parallel execution, to save the process states, and to restore 
a faulty process to the last known saved state. 
● The major challenge so far has been to perform a recovery during the message exchange 
between processes running in different cores. The implemented solution performs the 
recovery in all the processes involved in the communication. New strategies are under 
investigation. 
In space applications, radiation effects may eventually result in permanent faults in SRAM 
FPGAs. Also, these devices suffer from ageing, which results in a natural physical degradation 
process. The ageing speed depends on several factors such as the technology maturity, 
manufacturing quality control, temperature and thermal shock, operating voltage, dimensions, and 
even design complexity. As the FPGA resources density keeps increasing and with the recent Fin 
Field-Effect-Transistor (FinFET) technology generation, reliability concerns, mainly due to the 
Negative Bias Temperature Instability (NBTI), become a relevant factor [ZHA14]. Faults created 
by NBTI do not radically damage the FPGA resources, but they increase their response time. A 
design flow has been developed in a past PhD Thesis in the group, and it is applied to FPGA 
designs using hardcore or softcore processors [MAR15]. The results show that the proposed

20 
 
mechanism can detect and tolerate permanent faults (hard errors), with only a slight increase in the 
amount of memory required by a processor. 
The research activities employing the BRAVE radiation-hardened FPGA, and also the basic 
infrastructure to be provided at UFSC to build a Brazilian endured FPGA, will take in account all 
problems identified in previous research works. 
 
3.2. Verification and validation 
In order to avoid unnecessary redesign efforts, testing and verification strategies used in 
previous projects will be adopted since the conception of the new architecture. Concomitantly with 
the SAToC components development, all VHDL implementations will be performed according to 
the DO-254 recommendations. After enough iterations, this research outcome will be a validation 
strategy, at the HDL abstraction level, for VHDL based designs. A challenging research topic is the 
definition of strategies and procedures for the verification and validation of modules described at 
the Hardware Description Language (HDL) abstraction level [SIL13] [DAB15] [YOR95] [KHA13] 
[DEL03] [LIT12], targeting the qualification of On-Board Computer (OBC) subsystems under 
development in HDL. There are already well defined procedures for qualifying software based 
subsystems, and the on-going research at UFSC, aims at the qualification of HDL based 
components, more exactly, a SAToC implemented in an FPGA. In order to avoid unnecessary 
redesign efforts, testing and verification strategies used in previous projects will be adopted since 
the conception of the new architecture. 
In a previous SpaceLab PhD Thesis, the Universal Verification Methodology (UVM) was 
used since the very beginning of the project in order to ensure the developed system correctness. 
The use of UVM in the Thesis had as a goal to organise the project, improve the development time, 
increase the system correctness, and to provide metrics for the comparison of different algorithms. 
The target system had a software part developed in C++, and a hardware part described in VHDL, 
targeting an FPGA implementation. An important contribution of the Thesis was that the same test 
benches could be applied to both early conceptual software-only implementations, and later 
optimised software-hardware hybrid systems, in a hardware-in-the-loop configuration. The 
simulator was also used to inject specific noise, in order to evaluate the system under some real-
world conditions. This methodology, with some improvements and adaptations, is now adopted in 
the on-going satellite missions in the group. 
Another validation technique already employed in several works in the group is the 
"hardware-in-the-loop" (HIL). There are on-going works employing HIL validation in actual 
hardware implementations of the CCSDS communications module. There is a port of the CCSDS 
for the Zynq device, validated using a Python/Simulink implementation, and another port for the 
BRAVE FPGA, validated using the Cortex equipment. 
 
3.3. Project management 
For the project's management it is adopted the same methodology successfully used in 
previous projects, which is heavily based on the preparation of project documentation. Verification 
and validation activities should be performed throughout the project's lifetime, and a proper 
documentation procedure must be adopted. In previous projects in the space field, the adopted

21 
 
methodologies were heavily based on the preparation of project documentation, and the following 
phases were considered: feasibility; preliminary definition; detailed definition; qualification and 
production; operation / utilisation; and disposal. In each phase data packages are produced, 
including not only source code and schematics, but also test procedures, setups, and all the 
documentation. There are several "review of design" meetings, where the data packages are 
delivered by the responsible group. The following review of design meetings are expected to be 
included in a design targeting a space application, but not all of them are needed in cubesat 
missions: MDR - Mission Definition Review; PRR - Preliminary Requirements Review; SRR - 
System Requirements Review; PDR - Preliminary Design Review; CDR - Critical Design Review; 
QR - Qualification Review; AR - Acceptance Review; ORR - Operational Readiness Review; and 
FRR - Flight Readiness Review. Considering the risk matrix shown in Figure 11, the following 
main risks have been identified: 
● Human resources availability (mainly PhD candidates) 
o Severity: 3 
o Probability: 2 
o Magnitude: 6 - Low risk 
o Mitigation: There are 5 on-going PhD theses on topics related to this proposal, 
and there are 5 Master students that are encouraged to develop their work in 
partnership with the PhD researchers. 
● Infrastructure availability (design tools and test equipment) 
o Severity: 4 
o Probability: 1 
o Magnitude: 4 - Low risk 
o Mitigation: SpaceLab has the appropriate infrastructure for the proposal, and 
there are also other research laboratories at UFSC and in the partner universities 
(in Brazil and abroad) able to provide any extra support. 
● Research grant availability 
o Severity: 4 
o Probability: 2 
o Magnitude: 8 - Medium risk 
o Mitigation: In the past three years SpaceLab received funding from the Brazilian 
Space Agency and from the European Space Agency to develop several satellites. 
Even though the funding for the next two years is already secured, we are always 
looking for new funding opportunities in Brazil and abroad.

4. Expected results 
 
The proposed research has two main topics: the definition of an ecosystem at 
SpaceLab/UFSC to support other groups in the development of a radiation-hardened FPGA device; 
and the research and development of components to be used in the conception of a SAToC. Both 
topics share the idea of having several modules with well defined  interfaces, and that could be 
easily connected to each other in order to allow a designer to build the most appropriate system 
(rad-hard FPGA or SAToC), according to the application requirements. Both are quite complex 
designs, and EDA tools are used for the respective system generation. At the moment, the rad-hard 
FPGA is in a conception phase, and the expected results in the next few years would be to establish 
a network of partners in the VLSI design chain, interested in supporting this sort of project. The 
group's expertise will be used to guide the partners in the development of the libraries, and also to 
develop the components itself. It is expected that with this new initiative in place at SpaceLab, 
Master and PhD students will be attracted to develop their research in this field. 
On the other hand, as shown in Figure 5, the SAToC is a research topic well established in 
the group, with several components already developed in recent years, and ready to fly in a satellite 
mission. It is important to have in mind that the components shown in Figure 5 may be all in the 
same SAToC only as a proof-of-concept. In an actual mission, just the needed ones will be used to 
build the SAToC, according to the requirements. The basic components shown in the block diagram 
in Figure 5 are of special interest for this proposal. There are on-going Master and PhD theses in 
subjects related to those components, which are briefly described next: 
● Processor cores - There are several processors in use in the research group at UFSC. 
The LEON3 processor has been conceived by Gaisler targeting space applications, and it 
has been investigated and used in a couple of Master and PhD works at UFSC, mainly in 
the context of reliability enhancement. In recent years, the group started to migrate to 
RISC-V, performing studies targeting possible modifications in the processor’s 
architecture, aiming for energy consumption efficiency. These modifications cannot 
result in any changes in the ISA, as the idea is to keep the architecture fully compatible 
with the RISC-V compilers. The REON processor is a RISC-V and LEON3 based 
implementation, and it is an interesting architecture to be investigated also. The ARM 
processor is available in the Zynq device, used in some of the on-going modules, and it 
is available also in the NG-Large and NG-Ultra FPGAs from NanoXplore, which are the 
rad-hard FPGAs in use in the GOMX-5 mission. 
● Operating System - The target space application requires a real-time operating system. 
Currently, the on-going research projects at SpaceLab/UFSC target RTEMS (LEON3 
implementations) and FreeRTOS (RISC-V and MSP430 implementations). RTEMS is 
used in the INPE related projects, in order to control the tasks execution, assuring that 
they meet the proposed deadlines, according to the OBC requirements. INPE's original 
mono-processed ADCS software has been developed targeting the ERC32 (SPARC) 
processor running on RTEMS. Thus, in the same way that the ERC32 software was 
ported to the LEON3 architecture, the operating system must be ported as well. 
Furthermore, real-time operating systems are used in other target processors already 
discussed.

subject of another PhD Thesis at UFSC under my supervision finished in 2021: “MPI

● OBDH/OBC modules (software and hardware) - Several OBC modules have already 
been developed at UFSC as, for instance, the CCSDS protocol for the TC/TM 
communication system, the Energy Power System, the Star Tracker, the On-Board Data 
Handling, among other sensors and actuators. Some of these modules, including their 
software and hardware components, have already been ported to the proposed 
architecture, where the tasks to be executed by the processor cores, through parallel or 
distributed execution, have been validated. Other tasks will depend on ADCS/OBC 
subsystems to be completed. 
● ADCS (software and hardware) - This is a research field that has not been well 
explored in the research group. However, there has been very good work in the Star 
Tracker topic, including a Master and a PhD Thesis [MAR20a]. In the Master, the target 
was to have a system with smaller memory and processing power usage during the 
attitude determination processing, targeting CubeSats. The developed system identifies 
star centroids directly from the star pixel data stream of the CSI-2 bus, commonly 
employed in image sensors of Star Trackers. The CSI-2 bus is connected to an FPGA 
that filters the star pixels and transmits them to a processor, which computes the 
centroids of the respective image using a Kalman Filter. The former PhD student is now 
a researcher at Kyutech, in Japan, and this collaboration opened several research 
opportunities in the field. 
● Communication - The block diagram in Figure 5 shows several communication options, 
including a telecommand and telemetry (TT&C) for the service platform, and a high data

o VHDL component to implement CCSDS over DVB-S2, for high bandwidth 
image telemetry

● The Electrical Power System (EPS) - The EPS initiative is an important on-going 
activity in the group, with solid scientific contributions [KES20] [MAR21a] [RIG21a] 
[SLO16a] [SLO18]. Several EPS prototypes, engineering models, and a flight model 
have been designed and developed for the FloripaSat-1 mission, and have been adapted

25 
 
to on-going missions. It is one of the modules developed at SpaceLab with flight 
heritage, as a result of the FloripaSat-1 successful mission. An innovative task 
scheduling algorithm based on energy harvesting maximisation policy was developed in 
two PhD Theses [MAR21a][SLO16a][SLO18]. The energy harvesting circuit is 
mathematically modelled and a scheduling algorithm is designed to keep solar panels 
working close to their maximum power point by triggering tasks in the appropriate form. 
Tasks execution affects battery voltage, which is coupled to the solar panels through a 
protection circuit. A software based Perturb and Observe strategy allows defining the 
tasks to be triggered. A test apparatus was used to emulate solar irradiance variation, 
considering the satellite movement around the Earth. Tests have been conducted to show 
that the scheduling algorithm improves the CubeSat energy harvesting capability by up 
to 8.46% in a single orbit cycle, in comparison with the CubeSat operating without the 
scheduling algorithm. The solar panels and batteries behaviours, with and without the 
scheduling algorithm are shown in Figure 6. 
● Reconfigurable devices (FPGAs) - The main goal is to have the whole architecture 
(satellite modules) implemented as a single chip, and also to allow system 
reconfiguration. The target device is an FPGA, and the requirements of a space mission 
represent an important challenge. An SRAM based FPGA, for instance, is susceptible to 
radiation effects, and this is one of the restrictions for its use in this type of application. 
Another important issue is the amount of static power consumed by the SRAM 
technology, when considering its large die area usage. An important on-going work in 
the group targets the radiation-hardened FPGAs from Nanoxplore, which is a French 
company producing ITAR free devices. A payload based on this FPGA has been 
developed for the FloripaSat missions, in cooperation with ESA/ESTEC (The 
Netherlands), and Nanoxplore, who provided an FPGA kit, the software licence, and 
FPGA devices for radiation campaigns and for the satellite's flight model.

7. Final remarks, relevance and impact of the project 
 
In this proposal, two lines of research with different characteristics are described. Initially, in 
Section 2.1, the work in the area of development of a radiation-tolerant FPGA device was 
described. As discussed earlier, the objective is not to develop the FPGA device, but to create at 
UFSC, and in the laboratory, the necessary culture to support other groups in Brazil working on 
similar initiatives. It is a project of great complexity, which needs a network of partner institutions 
to reach the final objective. It is a long-term research topic at UFSC, focused on the development of 
basic libraries to be used in EDA tools for the generation of radiation-tolerant technology. The 
development at UFSC is performed by graduate students, in the context of their theses, and 
supervisioned by myself and colleagues from different microelectronics areas. The design of a rad-
hard FPGA is a good integrative project to bring different groups and researchers together, as well 
as the design houses. 
The other line of research introduced in this proposal targets the research and development 
of components to create a Satellite-on-a-chip (SAToC), and it can be entirely developed in 
SpaceLab, since there is already a well-established culture in the laboratory after about two decades 
of work in the area. All this acquired knowledge is shared with partners, and also transferred to the 
industry. This sharing also results in the absorption of new technologies provided by the laboratory 
partners, as well as the receipt of funds for the development of new activities. Even with the full 
understanding of the technology necessary for the development of the SAToC, as part of the 
methodology, partners from UFSC and other institutions work in a collaborative way in research 
and development activities, as described in the text of the proposal. 
A full satellite mission (FloripaSat-1) has been developed in the SpaceLab facilities, and 
several subsystems have been designed as a result of PPGEEL and PPGESE theses. At the moment, 
there are five on-going satellite missions at SpaceLab, simultaneously, which demonstrates our 
organisation and capability to develop this sort of work. The first small satellite mission was 
SERPENS-I, led by UnB, and SpaceLab was a member of the consortium of universities that 
supported the mission. After that, we developed the FloripaSat-1 (https://floripasat.ufsc.br/) 
mission, launched in December 2019, and we are working now in the GOLDS-UFSC (2U cubesat), 
Catarina-1a (2U cubesat), Catarina-1B (2U cubesat), GOMX-5 (one of the payloads for this 12U 
cubesat), and Aldebaran-I (1U cubesat from UFMA, which uses the boards hardware/software from 
FloripaSat-1). There are other missions in Brazil and abroad using our FloripaSat mission software 
and hardware, as it is one of the few satellite missions in the world, with flight heritage, available as 
open source (https://github.com/floripasat and https://github.com/spacelab-ufsc/). 
The expertise in the development of the satellites’ modules has been used in the design of 
the SAToC components. The satellite missions are excellent platforms not only to verify the correct 
functionality of the components, but also to perform an “actual system flight proven through 
successful mission operations”, as defined by NASA’s Technology Readiness Level definition. All 
FloripaSat-1 hardware and software components are TRL 9, and this flight heritage is used directly 
in the porting to the SAToC components. The experience acquired in the design of the radiation 
tolerance strategies is used also to understand the needs of the proposed radiation-hardened FPGA 
design.

42 
 
As mentioned before, Brazil used to be one of the few countries in the world using and 
producing space technology. In recent years, with the scale of new technologies, and the low cost 
access to space, there is a continuous increase in the amount of players in this field. As a result, the 
research and development of some key subsystems is crucial for the country to keep competitive in 
this field. For instance, few countries master the technology to develop attitude control systems for 
aerospace applications. This is one of the components to be developed for the proposed SAToC, 
allowing new research initiatives and original contributions in this field. 
The conception of a satellite-on-a-chip as a whole is, by itself, an important innovation 
resulting in several technological advances. The research works to be conducted by PPGEEL 
students under my supervision at SpaceLab, in order to come up with the embedded satellite 
modules described in this proposal, will represent important scientific contributions, allowing 
positioning the research in the state-of-the-art. 
Considering the previous experience obtained in works in the field developed at UFRGS, 
PUCRS, Oxford (UK), Sussex (UK), INPE, UFSC, UNSW (Australia), LIRMM (France), and the 
ESA/ESTEC (The Netherlands), it is straightforward that the innovative architectures and 
methodologies conceived during the research must be qualified in actual satellite flights. This is not 
only the best way to prove a space technology, but also the expected research outcome from the 
industry perspective. A novel satellite-on-a-chip based on a multi-processed architecture will not be 
employed in a future satellite, while it is not fully qualified. An effective way to qualify this 
architecture is a low cost (launching and maintainability) satellite as, for instance, the Cubesat 
standard.  
At SpaceLab, there are on-going theses related to on-board computer architectures of 
satellites, and also Cubesats. There are also works, at the Master and undergraduate levels. An 
interesting work investigates the setup of a space-Earth network of ground stations and satellites. A 
constellation of Cubesats may have dozens of satellites connected in an ad hoc topology, and they 
may be connected to ground stations around the globe, which are considered also as nodes of the 
network. Cubesat constellations represent an important motivation for the research in the fields 
described in this text, and also in the related technologies, including, but not limited to: soft-
processors; multi-core systems; unconventional arithmetic; interconnection infra-structures; image 
processing; sensors & actuators; ground segment; reliability improvement; verification & 
validation; radiation effects; PCB design; TT&C; ADCS; EPS; OBDH; GNSS; DVB-S2; RTOS; 
5G; SDR; and SDN. The proposed work may profit from several on-going research and 
development agreements between Europe and Brazil. For instance, recently Thales-Alenia has 
produced and delivered a geostationary satellite under a Brazilian contract, with the condition of 
performing technology transfer activities from Europe to Brazil. This could result in further 
international collaboration in the aerospace field, targeting not only the development, integration 
and testing of small satellites, but also teaching and consulting activities. 
GOLDS-UFSC and Catarina Constellation satellites are good options for, at least, a first 
flight of the proposed SAToC. A PC104 style Cubesat FPGA PCB has been designed in the 
previous period of the PQ scholarship, in order to embed some of the already developed SAToC 
modules. For the new proposal, a simplified version of an attitude determination control system 
(ADCS) can receive positioning (movement) information from MEMs sensors (gyroscopes, 
accelerometers, and magnetometer), and activate the actuators (magnetic torquer rods and reaction 
wheels). A prototype for this module is under development in a partnership between UFSC and 
UEL. This FPGA board can be part of a Catarina Constellation satellite, which would, for instance,

43 
 
change the satellite's pointing direction from time to time, in order not only to qualify the proposed 
SAToC, but also to perform some useful manoeuvre during the flight. The stricter energy 
requirements of a Cubesat [SLO16a][FRO14] represent a great challenge to stress the limits of the 
proposed SAToC software and hardware functionalities. The SpaceLab satellite may have the first 
MPSoC system in orbit around the Earth, implementing the satellite's ADCS functionalities. 
The ADCS and the CCSDS module described in this proposal are on-board systems of 
major interest for the Brazilian space program. INPE has been working for several years on 
their multi-mission platform, and our CCSDS module has been originally developed for 
INPE's satellites. This proposal represents a great opportunity to update the components 
architectures, improving their processing speed, and increasing their reliability figures. A 
Cubesat mission of a SAToC with these components, will provide flight heritage for these 
modules, proving not only the concepts, but certifying the technology for use in a standard 
(larger size) Brazilian satellite. 
Another main contribution would be the integration of a software-defined radio (SDR) 
allowing the mission to have 5G communication capabilities. As discussed before, the new 5G 
standard has a specific service for IoT applications, and a SAToC implementing this sort of 
communication platform will be a valuable tool for the next generation of always connected 
devices. 
Other microelectronics initiatives by the author of this proposal include a RISC-V processor 
that has been used in the editions of the hardware description language course at PPGEEL/UFSC 
(CAPES 6) since 2019 (https://gse.ufsc.br/bezerra/?page_id=2682). Basically, the students are 
asked to implement architectures based on the RISC-V description, and they perform the synthesis 
targeting FPGA technology. In collaboration with other microelectronics colleagues from UFSC, an 
approach similar to the rad-hard FPGA one described in this proposal is adopted in order to 
develop, manufacture, and encapsulate a chip with RISC-V, aimed at producing RISC-V based 
development kits for teaching activities in undergraduate and graduate courses, such as 
microprocessors, and embedded systems programming. Other architectures have been used in the 
past as, for instance, the zr16s08 microcontroller from Santa Maria Design House 
((https://gse.ufsc.br/bezerra/?page_id=2212)). The students receive just the device’s datasheet, and 
they describe its functionality in a HDL targeting an FPGA family. They demonstrate the working 
version on an FPGA board available in the teaching laboratory. It was an interesting approach as the 
course usually has students employed by the electronics industry (e.g. Intelbras, Khomp, Digitro) 
and they found the microcontroller from SMDH a good alternative to be used in the companies 
products, mainly because of the government tax incentives. 
For the near future, there are research proposals under preparation to be submitted to 
European/Brazilian joint calls. It is a high value-added field, and there are several funding 
opportunities available for researchers and companies. The research proposal will result in an 
advanced architecture to be used in future space missions, and it may also produce several spin-off 
research and product possibilities. The multidisciplinary nature of the proposal is an inspiration for 
unfolding new proposals in the medium and long terms. In addition, the project could profit from 
new technologies, and could be useful also in other application fields as, for instance, autonomous 
robots guidance, self-driving cars, among others. 
This is a multidisciplinary research topic, which would be of interest to several laboratories 
at UFSC, but mainly to the ones with research units in the SpaceLab network. SpaceLab is under

44 
 
the Technological Centre (CTC) structure, and it has research units in laboratories belonging to the 
Electrical Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, and Control and Automation Engineering 
departments. This network is expanding to other CTC departments, as Computer Science and 
Physics. There is also a strong cooperation with the Aerospace Engineering course at UFSC 
Campus Joinville. This proposal also receives scientific contribution, and represents a continuous 
collaboration with researchers from LIRMM/CSU (France), UNSW (Australia), Morehead (USA), 
Kyutech (Japan), Nottingham (UK), and ESTEC (The Netherlands).
